burial.plot_notes: West Cemetery, Amherst MA, Dickinson family plot

cross_connections.description: Close friend and correspondent; sent him 40+ poems and 50+ letters, mostly 1861-1862; he was the only non-family member she spoke to at her father's 1874 funeral.

cross_connections.display_label: the geographer who published her unseen poems in 1945
her Amherst neighbor and first editor
another American poet who shattered poetic convention

narrative_blocks.header: Sent forty poems and fifty letters to Bowles
narrative_blocks.body: She met Samuel Bowles III at her brother Austin's home in Amherst around 1858 and over the following decade sent him approximately forty of her poems and fifty letters — the majority during 1861-1862, her most prolific period. He published a handful of her poems anonymously in the Springfield Republican but never any from the private correspondence she sent him directly.

narrative_blocks.header: Eleven-year breach repaired at her father's funeral
narrative_blocks.body: She and Bowles had a falling-out in 1863 that lasted eleven years, despite his continued visits to her brother Austin next door at The Evergreens. The breach ended when her father Edward died in 1874 — Bowles was the only non-family member she spoke to at the funeral, and she wrote thanking him for his kindness shortly after.

narrative_blocks.header: Mourned Bowles two years before her own death
narrative_blocks.body: Bowles died in January 1878; she wrote to his widow Mary, 'Dear Mr Sam is very near, these midwinter days. When purples come on Pelham, in the afternoon we say Mr Bowles's colors.' She herself died eight years later in May 1886.

naming_inspiration.description: Given his father Calvin Murray's name — his father had died when Calvin Nelson was only two years old.
